[Page 7]
Medical IoT and telemedicine are fast-growing domains, driven by IoT, AI/ML, and sensing technologies, but they impose strict design constraints
due to HIPAA and FDA regulations, data security requirements, and
unreliable Internet connectivity in elderly care scenarios .
The system architecture comprises three layers :
1. Far Edge Layer : Patient -side wearable and medical -grade sensing devices
that securely collect physiological data and communicate with local
infrastructure .
2. Near -Edge PAN–WAN Layer : A secure, tamper -resistant local gateway
deployed at the patient’s location, providing networking, edge computing, device management, situational awareness, and temporary secure data
storage during connectivity failures .
3. Cloud Layer : A centralized platform for large -scale data aggregation, storage, analytics, dashboards, and rule-based alerting to support clinicians
managing hundreds of patients and rapid emergency response .
Together, these layers enable a secure, scalable, and regulation -compliant
sensor -to-cloud medical IoT system .

[Page 8]
•From a block diagram perspective, the wearable will consist of as few components as possible to minimize space and
weight while conserving as much power as possible .
•Here we elect to use a very power -efficient microcontroller with a Bluetooth 5 radio (Bluetooth Low Energy – BLE) .
The BLE radio will serve as the PAN communication to the PAN -WAN hub.
•BLE 5 has a range of up to 100 meters
(or further when LE Long -Range Mode is enabled)

[Page 12]
•IoT in Hospitals: Wearables, ICU sensors, glucose monitors, ECG, BP sensors.
• Azure IoT Hub: Secure gateway for all medical devices.
•Data Flow: Devices → IoT Hub → Stream Analytics → Alerts → Doctors.
•Stream Analytics: Real-time processing of patient vitals.
•Threshold Rules: Medical limits defined by doctors.
•Service Bus: Reliable message delivery.
•Logic Apps: Triggers alerts to hospital systems.
•Dynamics: Hospital CRM, patient records, emergency tickets.
•Azure SQL: Stores patient history
•Dashboards: Doctor & nurse real -time dashboards
•Remote Monitoring: Home patients connected to hospital
•Benefits: Early alerts, reduced mortality, efficiency

IoT Architecture
and
Core IoT Modules

[Page 14]
•The edge computing and IoT ecosphere starts with the simplest of sensors located
in the remotest corners of the earth and translates analog physical effects into
digital signals (the language of the Internet).
•Data then takes a complex journey through wired and wireless signals , various
protocols, natural interference, and electromagnetic collisions, before arriving in
the ether of the Internet.
•From there, packetized data will traverse various channels arriving at a cloud or
large data center .
•The strength of IoT is not just one signal from one sensor, but the aggregate of
hundreds, thousands, potentially millions of sensors, events, and devices .

[Page 15]
•Sensing and power : We cover the transformation of physical to digital sensing, power systems, and
energy storage .
•Data communication : We delve into the communication of devices using near-meter, near-kilometer, and extreme -range communication systems and protocols as well as networking and information theory .
•Edge computing : Edge devices have multiple roles from routing, to gateways, edge processing and
cloud -edge (fog) interconnect . We examine the role of the edge and how to successfully build and
partition edge machines . We also look at communication protocols from the edge to the cloud .
•Compute, analytics and machine learning : We then examine dataflow through cloud and fog
computing, as well as advanced machine learning and complex event processing .
•Threat and security : The final content investigates security and the vulnerability of the largest attack
surface on earth .

[Page 17]
As illustrated in the preceding figure, here are some of the components within an IoT/edge
solution that we will study :
•Sensors, actuators, and physical systems : Embedded systems, real-time operating systems, energy -harvesting sources, micro -electro -mechanical systems (MEMs) .
•Sensor communication systems : Wireless personal area networks (WPANs) reach from 0 cm
to 100 m. Low-speed and low-power communication channels, often non-IP based, have a
place in sensor communication .
•Local area networks (LANs) : Typically, IP-based communication systems such as 802.11 Wi-
Fi used for fast radio communication, often in peer-to-peer or star topologies .
•Aggregators, routers, gateways : Embedded systems providers, cheapest vendors
•Wide area networks (WANs) : Cellular network providers using LTE or Cat M1, satellite
network providers, low-power wide -area network (LPWAN) providers like Sigfox or LoRa .

[Page 18]
• Edge computing : Distributing computing from on-premise data centres and cloud to closer to the
sources of data (sensors and systems) . This is to eliminate latency issues, enhance response times
and real-time systems, mitigate connectivity gaps, and establish system redundancy .
• Cloud : Infrastructure as a service provider, platform as a service provider, database
manufacturers, streaming and batch processing manufacturers, data analytics packages, software as
a service provider, data lake providers, and machine learning services .
• Data analytics : As the information propagates to the cloud massive, dealing with volumes of data
and extracting value is the job of complex event processing, data analytics, and machine learning
techniques .
• Security : End-to-end security from edge hardening, protocol security, to encryption . Security
will impact every component, from physical sensors to the CPU, as well as digital hardware, radio
communication systems, and the communication protocols themselves .

[Page 19]
IoT versus machine -to-machine versus SCADA
•One common area of confusion in the IoT world is what separates it from the technologies that define
machine to machine (M2M).
•Before IoT became part of the mainstream vernacular, M2M was the hype .
•Well before M2M, SCADA (supervisory control and data acquisition) systems were the mainstream of
interconnected machines for factory automation .
M2M: It is a general concept involving an autonomous device communicating directly to another
autonomous device . Autonomous refers to the ability of the node to instantiate and communicate
information with another node without human intervention .
The form of communication is left open to the application . It may very well be the case that an M2M
device uses no inherent services or topologies for communication . This leaves out typical Internet
appliances used regularly for cloud services and storage .
An M2M system may communicate over non-IP based channels as well, such as a serial port or custom
protocol .

[Page 20]
IoT: IoT systems may incorporate some M2M nodes (such as a Bluetooth mesh using non-IP communication), but they aggregate data at an edge router or gateway .
An edge appliance like a gateway or router serves as the entry point onto the Internet . Alternatively, some
sensors with more substantial computing power can push the Internet networking layers onto the sensor itself .
SCADA : This term refers to supervisory control and data acquisition . These are industrial control systems that
have been used in factory, facility, infrastructure and manufacturing automation since the 1960 s.
They typically involve programmable logic controllers (PLCs) that monitor or controls various sensors and
actuators on machinery .
SCADA systems are distributed and only recently have been connected to Internet services . This is where
Industry 2.0 and the new growth of manufacturing is taking place .
These systems use communication protocols such as ModBus , BACNET, and Profibus .

[Page 21]
•Connecting sensors and edge devices to the Internet enabled even simple devices to leverage cloud
services .
•Earlier, embedded systems lacked global connectivity, scalable storage, and advanced analytics .
•Advances in cloud computing, mobile/wireless networks, and low-cost energy technologies (e.g., Li-ion
batteries) removed these barriers .
•Machine learning transformed raw sensor data into actionable insights .
•The convergence of these technologies elevated IoT beyond basic M2M communication .
•Without this convergence, systems would remain isolated, point -to-point M2M solutions

[Page 22]
The value of a network and Metcalfe's and Beckstrom's laws
Robert Metcalfe in 1980 formulated the concept that the value of any network is proportional to the square of connected
users of a system . In the case of IoT, "users" may mean sensors or edge devices with some form of communication .

[Page 22]
The value of a network and Metcalfe's and Beckstrom's laws
Robert Metcalfe in 1980 formulated the concept that the value of any network is proportional to the square of connected
users of a system . In the case of IoT, "users" may mean sensors or edge devices with some form of communication .

[Page 23]
Metcalfe's law: The value of a network is represented as proportional to N^2. The cost of each node is
represented as kN where k is an arbitrary constant . In this case, k represents a constant of $10 per IoT edge
sensor . The key takeaway is the crossover point occurs rapidly due to the expansion of value and indicates when
this IoT deployment achieves a positive ROI.A graphical model helps to
understand the interpretation
as well as the crossover
point, where a positive return
on investment (ROI) can be
expected :

[Page 24]
•Metcalfe's law does not account for service degradation in cases in which service degrades as the number of users and/or data
consumption grows, but the network bandwidth does not.
•Metcalfe's law also doesn't account for various levels of network service, unreliable infrastructure (such as 4G LTE in a
moving vehicle), or bad actors affecting the network (for example, denial of service attacks) .

[Page 25]
•Beckstrom’s Law values a network by summing the value of all transactions made by all
connected devices .
•The true impact of a network is measured by the cost to users if the network goes down .
•This approach gives a more realistic estimate of IoT system value than just counting devices or
connections .
•The hardest part to model is the benefit (B) of each transaction . Some sensor events may have very
small impact (e.g., missing temperature data for an hour) . Other sensor failures can have huge
consequences (e.g., a dead water sensor leading to flooding and inventory loss) . Therefore, even tiny
IoT data points can be extremely valuable depending on the situation .
•An architect's first step in building an IoT solution should be to understand what value they are
bringing to what they are designing . In the worst case, an IoT deployment becomes a liability and
actually produces negative value for a customer .

[Page 26]
IoT & Edge Architecture
•IoT systems are far more complex and interdependent than traditional IT due to massive scale
and highly diverse technologies .
•Architects must make careful end-to-end design choices that affect scalability, performance, reliability, and cost.
•There are hundreds of IoT service providers and many PAN, LAN, and WAN protocols, all
varying by region and use case.
•Choosing the wrong protocol (e.g., PAN) can lead to poor signal quality and force costly fixes
like adding more nodes .

[Page 27]
Key architectural concerns include:
•Interference and connectivity (how data moves from edge to Internet)
•Resiliency and data loss cost
•Protocol selection (e.g., MQTT vs CoAP vs AMQP and cloud portability) •Decisions about where to process data (sensor, edge, or cloud) impact latency, bandwidth, and
operating cost.
•Edge/Fog computing helps reduce WAN traffic and improve response time.
•Choosing the wrong analytics engine can cause noisy results or exceed edge device resources .
•Cloud -to-sensor queries affect battery life of IoT devices .
•Security is critical because IoT creates a huge attack surface .
•With all combinations of devices, protocols, and edge/cloud options, there are over 1.5 million possible
IoT architectures .

[Page 28]
IoT design choices: The full spectrum of various levels of IoT architecture from the sensor to the cloud and back.

[Page 29]
Role of an IoT Architect
•An IoT architect is a horizontal, cross -domain expert who integrates hardware, networks, cloud, data, and security into one scalable, secure, and usable system .
•Unlike specialized roles (cloud, SaaS, data, etc.), the IoT architect connects all domains into a
single end-to-end architecture .
•The role targets large -scale, global deployments —from thousands to millions of edge devices
across continents and providers .
•The architect must think holistically, knowing that optimizing one part can negatively affect
another .

[Page 30]
Key responsibilities include asking system -wide questions such as:
➢Scalability : How many devices, how much data, and what networking and middleware are required?
➢Resilience : How should the system behave when connectivity is lost?
➢Provisioning & management : How does the cloud manage, update, and secure edge devices?
➢RF and environment : How will the system perform in noisy wireless conditions?
➢Software updates : How are sensors securely updated over the network?
➢Data value : What data should be collected, analyzed, and protected to improve customer outcomes?
➢Security : How are devices, communications, and transactions protected end-to-end?
The IoT architect must understand everything from theory (communication, information) to practical
deployment (edge, cloud, analytics, security) .

Part 2 – Data Communication
•Connectivity is the foundation of IoT without it, IoT does not exist .
•Communication theory : SNR, path loss, interference, Shannon’s law, and spectrum limits .
•Explores PANs (Bluetooth 5, Zigbee, Z-Wave), LANs (Wi-Fi, Thread, 6LoWPAN), and WANs (4G
LTE, Cat-1, Cat-NB, 5G, Multe -Fire) .
•Includes proprietary long-range networks like LoRaWAN and Sigfox .
•Focus : how data moves from sensors → WAN → cloud → back .Part 1 – Sensing & Power
•IoT begins with physical events (motion, temperature, health, machine state ).
•Sensors can generate tiny bits or huge data streams (e.g., audio, video ).
•Advances in MEMS and Moore’s law have made sensors tiny and cheap .
•Power and energy are critical : billions of sensors consume enormous energy .
•Cloud -side design choices can strongly affect edge power consumption .IoT System:

•IoT creates the largest cyber -attack surface ever.
•Devices operate in public, remote, mobile, and even inside humans.
Real-world IoT attacks
* Enterprise and consumer threats
